event: Earliest Archaic (pre-Ceramic) Amerindian peoples settle the island, leaving shell middens and stone tools.
Arawak (Igneri) peoples migrating from South America establish ceramic-making, agricultural villages on the island.
Taino-related Arawak societies flourish, harvesting the island's salt ponds and naming it Soualiga, or 'Land of Salt'.
Carib (Kalinago) peoples expand through the Lesser Antilles, displacing or absorbing earlier Arawak inhabitants.
Christopher Columbus sights the island during his second voyage and names it San Martin after Saint Martin of Tours.
Dutch settlers establish Fort Amsterdam to exploit the island's lucrative salt pans for trade.
Spain captures the island and expels the Dutch and French to protect its Caribbean shipping interests.
Dutch forces led by Peter Stuyvesant fail to retake the island from Spain, and Stuyvesant loses his leg in the attack.
Spain abandons the island, and French and Dutch settlers partition it by the Treaty of Concordia, creating a lasting dual sovereignty.
Settlers begin large-scale cultivation of tobacco and later sugar, importing enslaved Africans to work the plantations.
Conflicts between the Dutch and English see control of the Dutch side briefly disrupted during Anglo-Dutch wars.
English forces raid and damage the island's settlements during the War of the Spanish Succession.
Sugar plantations expand dramatically, making the enslaved African population the majority of the island's inhabitants.
Philipsburg is founded on the Dutch side around a sheltered salt-pond harbor and becomes the main town.
Marigot grows as the principal town and administrative center of the French side of the island.
News of the French Revolution reaches the French side, stirring political upheaval over slavery and rights.
France and the Netherlands formally fix the boundary between their two halves of the island by treaty.
France abolishes slavery, freeing the enslaved on the French side and prompting many on the Dutch side to flee across the border.
The Netherlands abolishes slavery, emancipating the enslaved population on the Dutch southern half of the island.
The Dutch side is declared a duty-free port, laying the foundation for its later commercial and tourism economy.
An airstrip is built during World War II, the precursor to Princess Juliana International Airport.
The French side becomes part of the overseas department of Guadeloupe under France's postwar reorganization.
The Charter for the Kingdom of the Netherlands makes the Dutch side part of the autonomous Netherlands Antilles.
Princess Juliana International Airport expands, opening the island to mass air tourism from North America.
Tourism booms on both sides as hotels, casinos, and cruise traffic transform the island's economy.
The first official Saint Martin's Day on November 11 celebrates the shared heritage of the divided island.
Hurricane Luis devastates the island in September, destroying much of its infrastructure and tourism facilities.
Hurricane Lenny strikes from an unusual westward track, causing severe coastal flooding and damage.
Voters on the French side approve separation from Guadeloupe in a referendum on territorial autonomy.
The French northern half becomes the Collectivity of Saint-Martin, a separate French overseas collectivity.
The Netherlands Antilles is dissolved and the Dutch southern half becomes Sint Maarten, an autonomous country within the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
Category 5 Hurricane Irma devastates both sides of the island in September, causing massive destruction and dozens of deaths.
The COVID-19 pandemic halts cruise and air tourism, triggering a severe economic crisis on the tourism-dependent island.
Sint Maarten secures reconstruction and pandemic recovery funding tied to governance reforms with the Netherlands.
